items.q: Why does the colour of a transition metal complex change when ligands are exchanged?
items.correct: Different ligands cause different amounts of d-orbital splitting, so different wavelengths of light are absorbed.
items.misconception: The colour changes because the metal ion itself changes oxidation state.
items.random_wrong: The colour changes because the new ligand is itself coloured.
items.why: Students assume colour comes from the ligand or from a redox change; they miss that the same metal ion gives different colours depending on which ligands are bound.

items.q: What kind of bond forms between a ligand and a central metal ion in a complex?
items.correct: A dative (coordinate) covalent bond — the ligand donates a lone pair into an empty orbital on the metal.
items.misconception: The ligand forms an ionic bond with the metal because the metal is positively charged.
items.random_wrong: The ligand shares electrons equally with the metal as a normal covalent bond.
items.why: Students see the +ve metal and assume electrostatic ionic bonding, missing that ligands donate lone pairs into vacant d-orbitals.

items.q: Why are most transition metal complex ions coloured?
items.correct: Ligands split the d-orbitals into two energy levels; electrons absorb visible light to jump between them (d-d transitions), and the complementary colour is transmitted.
items.misconception: Transition metal ions are coloured because they have a half-full d-subshell which naturally absorbs light.
items.random_wrong: Transition metals are coloured because their nuclei emit photons in the visible range.
items.why: Students see 'd-block = coloured' as a label but miss that colour requires a partially filled d-subshell AND ligand-induced splitting — Sc3+ and Zn2+ are colourless because the d level is empty or full.

items.q: Why is EDTA complex very stable?
items.why: Correct answer: Chelate effect (entropy). “Bigger ΔH only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Pale blue -> yellow. “Blue -> red” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
